briefing.opening: Good morning, everyone. We will now begin the 2026-06-16 Climate Briefing.

sections.title: 🔴 Accelerating Impacts of the Climate Crisis and Increasing Vulnerability
sections.content: Cases of domestic cultural heritage being damaged by the direct impacts of climate change have been reported. The Stele for Buddhist Monk Nangwon at Borimsa Temple in Gangneung suffered damage due to a cold wave, demonstrating the vulnerability of cultural heritage to the climate crisis [1]. Particularly in Mongolian ger villages, 17 years of observation have shown that children suffer the most from the climate crisis [3, 10, 35, 36], and a tragic phenomenon of many climate refugees leaving the grasslands to move to 'garbage mountains' is also occurring [11, 34]. UNICEF has also warned that climate change threatens the health of nearly all children, emphasizing the vulnerability of children worldwide [54]. Furthermore, the global disaster landscape is intensifying, with the emergence of new and deadly microorganisms due to climate change [47], heatwaves being recognized as life-threatening disasters [9, 44], and increasing coastal risks [60]. There has even been a warning that if greenhouse gas emissions are not reduced, the entire country could transform into a 'subtropical' climate by the end of this century [12].

sections.title: 🌍🇰🇷 Policy Challenges and the Gap in Energy Transition
sections.content: Globally, policy efforts to address climate change are evident, alongside emerging challenges. In the United States, an order from the previous administration to remove signs related to climate change and Indigenous history was halted and restored by a federal judge [37, 48, 52, 53], and U.S. lawmakers are fighting against budget cuts to ocean observation programs [65]. However, the Louisiana governor signed a bill limiting climate change-related lawsuits [51], and 60% of Israeli local authorities were found to have no plans to cope with climate change impacts, highlighting policy shortcomings [40]. Domestically, high costs of renewable energy and a shortage of liquefied natural gas (LNG) supply are causing a double burden in securing power for AI data centers [29]. It has also been pointed out that in certain regions, nuclear power is familiar, but renewable energy still feels distant, indicating a gap in the energy transition [27]. Furthermore, news that major energy company Equinor withdrew its renewable energy capacity targets suggests the realistic difficulties of energy transition [68]. Meanwhile, in Canada, youth and organizations are filing lawsuits over the 'failure' of the climate crisis, urging policy accountability [56].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Climate Change Response and Innovative Efforts
sections.content: Amidst this crisis, various efforts and innovations to address climate change are actively underway both domestically and internationally. Several local governments in Korea are encouraging carbon neutrality practices by offering local currency benefits [14, 15, 18, 22, 24] and expanding citizen participation through carbon neutrality education and disaster preparedness training [16, 17, 19, 23]. In the renewable energy sector, a competition for predicting wind power generation using AI has been held [25], and European offshore wind power is transforming into a 'cash cow' generating economic value [26], securing technological advancement and economic viability. Notably, the first commercial Power-to-Liquid (PtL) plant, which produces fuel using only renewable energy and air, has commenced operations [28], and Korea Southern Power is expanding its investments in renewable energy and AI [31]. Internationally, scientists are suggesting that more coral reefs may survive climate change than previously expected [43], and innovative attempts by scientists to re-thicken Arctic sea ice are also gaining attention [66]. Furthermore, an analysis showing that Indian households could save billions of rupees annually by using energy-efficient air conditioners demonstrates the economic benefits of practical climate action [67].

sections.title: 📰 Efforts in Climate Change Communication and Awareness Raising
sections.content: Various attempts to enhance public understanding and communication regarding climate change are also ongoing. A survey revealed a gap between public concern about climate change and the actual volume of news coverage, suggesting the importance of effective information dissemination [58]. In California, a new attempt is underway to legislate a climate change education center [49], and in the Nashville area, efforts are continuing to collect personal experiences and stories related to climate change in the form of oral histories [45]. Additionally, the COP31 Climate Change Media Partnership Reporting Fellowship will be offered to creator journalists [59], expected to contribute to the qualitative improvement of climate change reporting. Countries are reviewing their climate change preparedness, with scientists and decision-makers discussing Estonia's readiness [57]. Interestingly, news that the misconception that climate change is an unfavorable issue for a particular political faction has been refuted by evidence demonstrates the potential for a shift in climate discourse [55].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights the severity of the climate crisis, alongside the co-existence of various efforts to overcome it. Sustained attention and cooperation from the international community, national governments, and civil society are essential for successful climate change response. This concludes the 2026-06-16 Climate Briefing.

This is the Ulaan Chuluut landfill, where hundreds of garbage trucks enter and exit daily. It is one of Mongolia's largest waste treatment facilities, a destination where urban waste accumulates. articles.title: US lawmakers fight Trump cuts to $386m ocean monitoring program: ‘supreme stupidity’
articles.description: Lisa Murkowski, a Republican senator, joins Democrats in bid to stop dismantling of Ocean Observatories InitiativeUS politics live – latest updatesA group of Democratic senators and one Republican, as well as two Democratic House committees, sent letters on Monday to the National Science Foundation asking it to reverse course on its plan to dismantle a sprawling ocean monitoring network, with House lawmakers going further and accusing the agency of acting illegally.The Ocean Observatories Initia

summary.phenomenon: Democratic senators and House committees, along with Republican Senator Lisa Murkowski, sent letters to the National Science Foundation (NSF) on Monday, requesting it reverse its plan to dismantle the $386 million Ocean Observatories Initiative (OII), an ocean monitoring network. House lawmakers further accused the NSF of acting illegally.
summary.cause: Although the article title mentions 'Trump's cuts' as the cause of the dismantling plan, the body of the text only refers to it as an NSF plan.
summary.outlook: While the efforts of the lawmakers may halt the dismantling of the ocean observation program, the final outcome or the impact on climate monitoring if dismantled was not mentioned in the article.

articles.original_title: ‘At first, the idea does sound crazy’: meet the scientists trying to refreeze the Arctic
articles.description: Sea ice is melting fast, worsening the climate crisis, but a bold attempt to rethicken it is showing early signs of success ‘This would have been a wild dream a year ago,” says Andrea Ceccolini, standing on Arctic sea ice just a 4-mile snowmobile ride from the Inuit town of Cambridge Bay, northern Canada. To his left are sky blue ponds of meltwater created in the last few days by a sun that no longer sets in the high north summer. To his right, the sea ice is still a brilliant white, the light d

summary.phenomenon: Arctic sea ice is melting rapidly, and meltwater ponds have been observed near Cambridge Bay, northern Canada, in recent days. Scientists are attempting to rethicken the sea ice.
summary.cause: The rapid melting of sea ice is worsening the climate crisis. The phenomenon of the sun not setting during the high-latitude summer was also mentioned.
summary.outlook: Attempts to rethicken Arctic sea ice are showing early signs of success. This could be a potential strategy for mitigating the climate crisis.
